---
title: "Yang-Mills Lattice Gauge Theory - William's Explorations"

bibliography: references.bib

# format:
#   html:
#     code-fold: true
# jupyter: python3
---

::: {.hidden}
$$
\newcommand\mcy{{\mathcal{Y}}}
\newcommand{\R}{\mathbb{R}}
\newcommand{\Tr}{\text{Tr}}
\def\*#1{\mathbf{#1}}
$$
:::

## June 3, 2026

Let $H = (H_e)_{e \in E_n} \in H(N)^{E_n}$ be a random 
Hermitian configuration distributed according to the law
$$
\frac{1}{Z_H(B_n)} \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} \lVert H_p \rVert^2 \right) d\lambda_{E_n^0}(H)
$$
where 
$$
d\lambda_{E_n^0}(H) = \bigotimes_{e \in E_n^0} \text{Unif}_{\{0\}}
\otimes \bigotimes_{e \in E_n^1} d \lambda(H),
$$
where $d\lambda(H)$ denotes Lebesgue measure on $H(N)$.
In particular, let 
$$
\Psi_H: \R^{N^2} \to H(N)
$$
be the map
\begin{equation}
\begin{gathered}
(y,z,w) = (\left(y_{j j}\right)_{1 \leq j \leq N}, 
\left(z_{j k}\right)_{1 \leq j<k \leq N}, \left(w_{j k}\right)_{1 \leq j<k \leq N}) \\
\mapsto \begin{bmatrix}
y_{11} & & & \frac{z_{jk} + iw_{jk}}{\sqrt{2}} \\
 & \ddots & &  \\
 & &  &  \\
\frac{z_{jk} - iw_{jk}}{\sqrt{2}} & & & y_{NN}
\end{bmatrix}.
\end{gathered}
\end{equation}

Then $d\lambda(H)$ is equal to 
the pushforward of $l_{N^2}$, $N^2$-dimensional
Lebesgue measure, by the map $\Psi_H$: 
$$
\lambda = \Psi_H^* l_{N^2} = l_{N^2} \circ \Psi_H^{-1}.
$$
In particular, if $f$ is some integrable function of $H \in H(N)$, 
then 
$$
\int f(H) d\lambda(H) = \int f(\Psi_H(y,w,z)) dl_{N^2}(y,w,z).
$$

Let $B \in \mathcal{B}(H(N)^{E_n})$ be a Borel 
subset of Hermitian configuration space. (Or do 
we need to take a cylinder set?)
Then 
$$
\mathbb P(H \in B) = \int_{B} \frac{1}{Z_H(B_n)} \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} \lVert H_p \rVert^2 \right) d\lambda_{E_n^0}(H).
$$
(How do you integrate against uniform measure on the $E_n^0$ edges?)
Fix $p \in P(B_n)$.
Then 
$$
H_p = H_{p_1} + H_{p_2} - H_{p_3} - H_{p_4},
$$
where the $p_i$ are the edges comprising the plaquette
$p$. We have separate parameters $(y_{p_i}, z_{p_i}, w_{p_i})$ 
for each of the four Hermitian matrices associated to the plaquette.
Note that $y_{p_i} \in \R^N$, $z_{p_i} \in \R^{N(N-1)/2}$, and $w_{p_i} \in \R^{N(N-1)/2}$. 
We write
$$
H_{p_i} = \Psi_H(y_{p_i}, z_{p_i}, w_{p_i})
$$
for each plaquette edge $p_i$. Then by linearity of $\Psi_H$, we have
$$
H_{p} = \Psi_H \left( y_{p_1} + y_{p_2} - y_{p_3} - y_{p_4}, z_{p_1} + z_{p_2} - z_{p_3} - z_{p_4}, w_{p_1} + w_{p_2} - w_{p_3} - w_{p_4} \right). 
$$

Then since $\Psi_H$ is an isometry between $\R^{N^2}$ equipped with the Euclidean norm
and $H(N)$ equipped with the Hilbert Schmidt norm, we 
have
\begin{equation}
\begin{gathered}
\lVert H_p \rVert^2 = \lVert (y_{p_1} + y_{p_2} - y_{p_3} - y_{p_4}, z_{p_1} + z_{p_2} - z_{p_3} - z_{p_4}, w_{p_1} + w_{p_2} - w_{p_3} - w_{p_4}) \rVert^2 \\
= \sum_{i=1}^N (y^{ii}_{p_1} + y^{ii}_{p_2} - y^{ii}_{p_3} - y^{ii}_{p_4})^2 + 
\sum_{1 \le i < j \le N} (z^{ij}_{p_1} + z^{ij}_{p_2} - z^{ij}_{p_3} - z^{ij}_{p_4})^2 \\
+ \sum_{1 \le i < j \le N} (w^{ij}_{p_1} + w^{ij}_{p_2} - w^{ij}_{p_3} - w^{ij}_{p_4})^2
\end{gathered}
\end{equation}
Then we write
\begin{equation}
\begin{gathered}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} \lVert H_p \rVert^2 =
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} \sum_{i=1}^N (y^{ii}_{p_1} + y^{ii}_{p_2} - y^{ii}_{p_3} - y^{ii}_{p_4})^2 + \\
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} \sum_{1 \le i < j \le N} (z^{ij}_{p_1} + z^{ij}_{p_2} - z^{ij}_{p_3} - z^{ij}_{p_4})^2 + \\
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} \sum_{1 \le i < j \le N} (w^{ij}_{p_1} + w^{ij}_{p_2} - w^{ij}_{p_3} - w^{ij}_{p_4})^2 \\
= \sum_{i=1}^N \sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (y^{ii}_{p_1} + y^{ii}_{p_2} - y^{ii}_{p_3} - y^{ii}_{p_4})^2 + \\
\sum_{1 \le i < j \le N} \sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (z^{ij}_{p_1} + z^{ij}_{p_2} - z^{ij}_{p_3} - z^{ij}_{p_4})^2 + \\
\sum_{1 \le i < j \le N} \sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (w^{ij}_{p_1} + w^{ij}_{p_2} - w^{ij}_{p_3} - w^{ij}_{p_4})^2 \\
= \sum_{i=1}^N \sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (y_{p}^{ii})^2 + \\
\sum_{1 \le i < j \le N} \sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (z_p^{ij})^2 +\\
\sum_{1 \le i < j \le N} \sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (w_p^{ij})^2.
\end{gathered}
\end{equation}
Then we have
\begin{equation}
\begin{gathered}
\exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} \lVert H_p \rVert^2 \right) = \prod_{1 \le i \le N} \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (y_{p}^{ii})^2 \right) \times \\
\prod_{1 \le i < j \le N} \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (z_p^{ij})^2 \right) \times \\
\prod_{1 \le i < j \le N} \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (w_p^{ij})^2 \right).
\end{gathered}
\end{equation}
Next we have
\begin{equation}
\begin{gathered}
d\lambda_{E_n^0}(H) = \prod_{e \in E_n^1} d\lambda(H_e) \\
= \prod_{e \in E_n^1} d\Psi_H^* l_{N^2}(H_e)
\end{gathered}
\end{equation}
Fixing an edge $e \in E_n^1$, we have 
\begin{equation}
\begin{gathered}
\int f(H_e) d\Psi_H^* l_{N^2}(H_e) = \int f(\Psi_H(y_e, z_e, w_e)) dl_{N^2}(y_e, z_e, w_e),
\end{gathered}
\end{equation}
so if we iterate this by freezing all other edge variables 
one at a time, we obtain
\begin{equation}
\begin{gathered}
\int \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} \lVert H_p \rVert^2 \right) d\lambda_{E_n^0}(H) = \\
\int \prod_{1 \le i \le N} \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (y_{p}^{ii})^2 \right) \times \\
\prod_{1 \le i < j \le N} \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (z_p^{ij})^2 \right) \times \\
\prod_{1 \le i < j \le N} \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (w_p^{ij})^2 \right) \prod_{e \in E_n^1} dl_{N^2}(y_e, z_e, w_e)
\end{gathered}
\end{equation}
But if we integrate first over the edges and then over the 
$N^2$ dimensions of $\R^{N^2}$, meaning use
\begin{equation}
\begin{gathered}
\prod_{e \in E_n^1} dl_{N^2}(y_e, z_e, w_e) = 
\prod_{1 \le i \le N} \prod_{e \in E_n^1} dl_1(y_e^{ii}) \otimes \\
\prod_{1 \le i < j \le N} \prod_{e \in E_n^1} dl_1(z_e^{ij}) \otimes \\
\prod_{1 \le i < j \le N} \prod_{e \in E_n^1} dl_1(w_e^{ij}),
\end{gathered}
\end{equation}
then we have 
\begin{equation}
\begin{gathered}
\int \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} \lVert H_p \rVert^2 \right) d\lambda_{E_n^0}(H) = \\
\prod_{1 \le i \le N} \int \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (y_{p}^{ii})^2 \right) \prod_{e \in E_n^1} dl_1(y_e^{ii}) \times \\
\prod_{1 \le i < j \le N} \int \exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (z_p^{ij})^2 \right) \prod_{e \in E_n^1} dl_1(z_e^{ij}) \times \\
\prod_{1 \le i < j \le N} \int 
\exp \left( - \frac{1}{2}
\sum_{p \in P(B_n)} (w_p^{ij})^2 \right) \prod_{e \in E_n^1} dl_1(w_e^{ij}) \\
\end{gathered}
\end{equation}

## June 2, 2026

Let $t \in \R^{E_n}$ be distributed according
to the probability distribution
$$
\frac{1}{Z_M(B_n)} \exp(-\frac{1}{2} M_n(t)) dl_{E_n^0, 1}(t),
$$
where 
$$dl_{E_n^0, 1}(t) = \bigotimes_{e \in E_n^0} \text{Unif}_{\{0\}}(t_e) \otimes 
\bigotimes_{e \in E_n^1} dl_1(t_e).
$$
This probability distribution is called 
lattice Maxwell theory on $B_n$ with boundary value 0.
In other words, $t_e$ is determinstically equal to 0
when $e \in E_n^0$ and is otherwise a random real number
with distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu_e, \sigma_e)$, 
where $\mu_e$ and $\sigma_e$ can be computed from $M_n$.
 
On the other hand, a Hermitian matrix $H \in H(N)$ can be 
parameterized using $N^2$ variables by writing the 
$(j, k)^{\text {th }}$ entry of $H$ as
$$
x_{j k}= \begin{cases}y_{j j} & \text { if } j=k, \\ \left(z_{j k}+\mathrm{i} w_{j k}\right) / \sqrt{2} & \text { if } j<k, \\ \left(z_{k j}-\mathrm{i} w_{k j}\right) / \sqrt{2} & \text { if } j>k,\end{cases}
$$
as illustrated by
$$
\begin{bmatrix}
y_{11} & & & \frac{z_{jk} + iw_{jk}}{\sqrt{2}} \\
 & \ddots & &  \\
 & &  &  \\
\frac{z_{jk} - iw_{jk}}{\sqrt{2}} & & & y_{NN}
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

Then the $N^2$ parameters are $\left(y_{j j}\right)_{1 \leq j \leq N},\left(z_{j k}\right)_{1 \leq j<k \leq N}$, and $\left(w_{j k}\right)_{1 \leq j<k \leq N}$.

Let $(t^{(jk)})_{1 \le j \le k \le N}$ be $N^2$
mutually independent lattice Maxwell fields, arranged 
in some arbitrary way. That is, for each $j,k$ with 
$1 \le j \le k \le N$, we have that $(t_e^{(jk)})_{e \in E_n}$
has the law above. 

Based on the definition of Lebesgue measure on $H(N)$, one sees
that the law of 
$H(t^{(jk)})$ is just the joint law of the $t^{(jk)}$, and is 
thus just the $N^2$ fold product of the law of scalar Maxwell theory
by independence of the $t^{(jk)}$.
